sections.title: 🧒 The Story — Why IAM Exists
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Imagine you own a large company with an office building.
The building has many rooms: server room, finance office, HR records, code lab.

WITHOUT IAM (no access control):
→ Every employee has a master key to every room
→ Junior developer accidentally deletes production database
→ Accountant can read confidential source code
→ Contractor who left still has full access
→ You have NO idea who went into which room when
→ DISASTER waiting to happen

WITH IAM (proper access control):
→ Each employee gets a BADGE (IAM User)
→ Badges are grouped by department (IAM Groups)
→ Each department's badge opens specific doors (IAM Policies)
→ Some tasks use temporary badges (IAM Roles)
→ Every door entry is logged (CloudTrail)

IAM = the badge + access control system for your entire AWS account
```

---

blocks.content:
Imagine you own a large company with an office building.
The building has many rooms: server room, finance office, HR records, code lab.

WITHOUT IAM (no access control):
→ Every employee has a master key to every room
→ Junior developer accidentally deletes production database
→ Accountant can read confidential source code
→ Contractor who left still has full access
→ You have NO idea who went into which room when
→ DISASTER waiting to happen

WITH IAM (proper access control):
→ Each employee gets a BADGE (IAM User)
→ Badges are grouped by department (IAM Groups)
→ Each department's badge opens specific doors (IAM Policies)
→ Some tasks use temporary badges (IAM Roles)
→ Every door entry is logged (CloudTrail)

IAM = the badge + access control system for your entire AWS account

revisionPoints: → Every employee has a master key to every room
→ Junior developer accidentally deletes production database
→ Accountant can read confidential source code
→ Contractor who left still has full access
→ You have NO idea who went into which room when
→ DISASTER waiting to happen
→ Each employee gets a BADGE (IAM User)
→ Badges are grouped by department (IAM Groups)

sections.title: 🌐 IAM is Global
sections.rawMarkdown:
### IAM Big Picture Diagram
```
User / app / AWS service
          │
          ▼
      IAM identity
          │
          ▼
    policy evaluation
   allow / deny / default deny
          │
          ▼
      AWS resource

MENTAL MODEL:
IAM sits between identities and actions as the gatekeeper.
```

```
IMPORTANT: IAM is a GLOBAL service.
→ IAM users, groups, roles, policies exist in ALL regions simultaneously
→ You do NOT choose a region when creating IAM resources
→ One IAM user can access resources in any region (if policy allows)
→ This is different from EC2, S3, RDS — those are regional

Contrast:
IAM User "alice"  → exists globally, no region
EC2 instance      → exists in us-east-1 specifically
```

---

blocks.content: ### IAM Big Picture Diagram

User / app / AWS service
          │
          ▼
      IAM identity
          │
          ▼
    policy evaluation
   allow / deny / default deny
          │
          ▼
      AWS resource

MENTAL MODEL:
IAM sits between identities and actions as the gatekeeper.

IMPORTANT: IAM is a GLOBAL service.
→ IAM users, groups, roles, policies exist in ALL regions simultaneously
→ You do NOT choose a region when creating IAM resources
→ One IAM user can access resources in any region (if policy allows)
→ This is different from EC2, S3, RDS — those are regional

Contrast:
IAM User "alice"  → exists globally, no region
EC2 instance      → exists in us-east-1 specifically

revisionPoints: → IAM users, groups, roles, policies exist in ALL regions simultaneously
→ You do NOT choose a region when creating IAM resources
→ One IAM user can access resources in any region (if policy allows)
→ This is different from EC2, S3, RDS — those are regional

sections.title: 🔑 Root Account vs IAM Users
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Root Account
```
Created when: you first sign up for AWS (email + password)
Has:          UNLIMITED access to everything in the account
Can:          never be restricted by any IAM policy
Risk:         if leaked → attacker has complete control forever

GOLDEN RULE: NEVER use root for daily work. EVER.

Root account should ONLY be used for:
→ Initial account setup (first 15 minutes)
→ Creating the first IAM admin user
→ Changing billing settings
→ Closing the AWS account
→ Restoring access if all admin IAM users are locked out

PROTECT ROOT:
✅ Enable MFA immediately
✅ Never create access keys for root
✅ Don't share root credentials with ANYONE
✅ Lock it away — pretend it doesn't exist
```

### IAM Users
```
Created by:  root or IAM admin
Has:         ZERO permissions by default (must explicitly grant)
Can do:      only what their policies allow
Risk:        limited — if compromised, can be disabled/deleted instantly

ONE USER PER PERSON — never share IAM users
Each person on your team = their own IAM user

Types of access an IAM user can have:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CONSOLE ACCESS (for humans):                               │
│   Username + Password + (MFA if enabled)                   │
│   Login at: https://ACCOUNT_ID.signin.aws.amazon.com       │
│                                                             │
│ PROGRAMMATIC ACCESS (for code/scripts):                    │
│   Access Key ID     → like a username (AKIAIOSFODNN7...)   │
│   Secret Access Key → like a password (wJalrX...)          │
│   Used by: AWS CLI, SDKs, CI/CD pipelines                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚠️ NEVER put access keys in code. NEVER push to GitHub.
   Bots scan GitHub 24/7 looking for leaked AWS keys.
   Average time from GitHub push to first unauthorized API call: 4 minutes.
```

---

blocks.content: ### Root Account

Created when: you first sign up for AWS (email + password)
Has:          UNLIMITED access to everything in the account
Can:          never be restricted by any IAM policy
Risk:         if leaked → attacker has complete control forever

GOLDEN RULE: NEVER use root for daily work. EVER.

Root account should ONLY be used for:
→ Initial account setup (first 15 minutes)

→ Changing billing settings
→ Closing the AWS account
→ Restoring access if all admin IAM users are locked out

✅ Enable MFA immediately

✅ Don't share root credentials with ANYONE
✅ Lock it away — pretend it doesn't exist

Can do:      only what their policies allow
Risk:        limited — if compromised, can be disabled/deleted instantly

ONE USER PER PERSON — never share IAM users
Each person on your team = their own IAM user

Types of access an IAM user can have:

│ CONSOLE ACCESS (for humans):                               │
│   Username + Password + (MFA if enabled)                   │
│   Login at: https://ACCOUNT_ID.signin.aws.amazon.com       │
│                                                             │
│ PROGRAMMATIC ACCESS (for code/scripts):                    │
│   Access Key ID     → like a username (AKIAIOSFODNN7...)   │
│   Secret Access Key → like a password (wJalrX...)          │
│   Used by: AWS CLI, SDKs, CI/CD pipelines                  │

→ Creating the first IAM admin user

→ Closing the AWS account

sections.title: 👥 IAM Groups
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
A group = a collection of IAM users that share permissions

WHY GROUPS EXIST:
You have 50 developers. Don't attach policy to each one.
→ Create "Developers" group → attach developer policy once
→ Add all 50 devs to group → all get permissions instantly
→ New dev joins → add to group → done
→ Dev leaves → remove from group → permissions revoked

RULES:
→ Groups contain USERS only (not other groups)
→ A user can be in MULTIPLE groups (gets combined permissions)
→ Users don't HAVE to be in a group (but should be)
→ No limit on number of groups
→ Max 10 groups per user (soft limit)

COMMON GROUPS IN REAL COMPANIES:
┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Group           │ Permissions                              │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Admins          │ Full AWS access (AdministratorAccess)    │
│ Developers      │ EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, CloudWatch         │
│ DevOps          │ EC2, ECS, CodePipeline, CloudFormation   │
│ DataScientists  │ S3 read, SageMaker, Athena, Redshift     │
│ ReadOnly        │ View everything, change nothing          │
│ Billing         │ Cost Explorer, billing only              │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

blocks.content:
A group = a collection of IAM users that share permissions

WHY GROUPS EXIST:
You have 50 developers. Don't attach policy to each one.
→ Create "Developers" group → attach developer policy once
→ Add all 50 devs to group → all get permissions instantly
→ New dev joins → add to group → done
→ Dev leaves → remove from group → permissions revoked

RULES:
→ Groups contain USERS only (not other groups)
→ A user can be in MULTIPLE groups (gets combined permissions)
→ Users don't HAVE to be in a group (but should be)
→ No limit on number of groups
→ Max 10 groups per user (soft limit)

COMMON GROUPS IN REAL COMPANIES:
┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Group           │ Permissions                              │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Admins          │ Full AWS access (AdministratorAccess)    │
│ Developers      │ EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, CloudWatch         │
│ DevOps          │ EC2, ECS, CodePipeline, CloudFormation   │
│ DataScientists  │ S3 read, SageMaker, Athena, Redshift     │
│ ReadOnly        │ View everything, change nothing          │
│ Billing         │ Cost Explorer, billing only              │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

revisionPoints: → Create "Developers" group → attach developer policy once
→ Add all 50 devs to group → all get permissions instantly
→ New dev joins → add to group → done
→ Dev leaves → remove from group → permissions revoked
→ Groups contain USERS only (not other groups)
→ A user can be in MULTIPLE groups (gets combined permissions)
→ Users don't HAVE to be in a group (but should be)
→ No limit on number of groups

sections.title: 🎭 IAM Roles
sections.rawMarkdown:
> **Key insight:** Roles are for AWS services and temporary access. They're actually MORE secure than users because credentials auto-expire.

```
IAM User  → permanent identity for a PERSON (long-lived credentials)
IAM Role  → temporary identity for a SERVICE or SITUATION

A role can be ASSUMED by:
→ AWS services (EC2 accessing S3, Lambda writing to DynamoDB)
→ Applications running inside AWS
→ External users (federated from company Active Directory)
→ Other AWS accounts (cross-account access)
→ Web identity (users logged in with Google/Facebook via Cognito)

When assumed → AWS generates TEMPORARY credentials (expire in 1–12 hours)
→ No password. No access keys. Auto-rotated. Much safer.
```

### Why Roles Instead of Access Keys for Services

```
THE WRONG WAY (never do this):
EC2 instance needs to read from S3
→ Create IAM user "app-service"
→ Generate access keys
→ SSH into EC2, store keys in ~/.aws/credentials or .env file
→ 
Problems:
  - Keys stored on disk → if EC2 hacked, attacker gets permanent keys
  - Keys don't expire → attacker has access until you manually rotate
  - Everyone on team now knows (or has seen) the keys
  - Keys in .env file → often accidentally committed to GitHub

THE RIGHT WAY (always do this):
EC2 instance needs to read from S3
→ Create IAM Role with S3 read permissions
→ Attach Role to EC2 instance
→ EC2 automatically gets temporary credentials via instance metadata
→ 
Benefits:
  - Credentials are temporary (expire in hours, auto-refreshed)
  - If EC2 is hacked, credentials expire soon anyway
  - No keys stored anywhere on disk
  - Zero code changes needed — AWS SDK automatically uses the role
```

### Common Role Examples

```
EC2 Instance Role:
  Trust: EC2 service can assume this role
  Policies: S3GetObject, DynamoDBFullAccess, CloudWatchLogsFullAccess

Lambda Execution Role:
  Trust: Lambda service can assume this role  
  Policies: AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole (CloudWatch logs)
            + whatever your function specifically needs

ECS Task Role:
  Trust: ECS tasks can assume this role
  Policies: specific services your container needs to call

Cross-Account Role:
  Trust: AWS account 111122223333 can assume this role
  Use case: Company A's team accesses Company B's resources
  Use case: CI/CD pipeline in one account deploys to another

CodePipeline Role:
  Trust: CodePipeline service can assume this role
  Policies: CodeBuild, S3, ECS deploy, CloudFormation
```

---

blocks.content: > **Key insight:** Roles are for AWS services and temporary access. They're actually MORE secure than users because credentials auto-expire.

IAM User  → permanent identity for a PERSON (long-lived credentials)
IAM Role  → temporary identity for a SERVICE or SITUATION

A role can be ASSUMED by:
→ AWS services (EC2 accessing S3, Lambda writing to DynamoDB)
→ Applications running inside AWS
→ External users (federated from company Active Directory)
→ Other AWS accounts (cross-account access)
→ Web identity (users logged in with Google/Facebook via Cognito)

When assumed → AWS generates TEMPORARY credentials (expire in 1–12 hours)
→ No password. No access keys. Auto-rotated. Much safer.
### Why Roles Instead of Access Keys for Services

THE WRONG WAY (never do this):
EC2 instance needs to read from S3
→ Create IAM user "app-service"
→ Generate access keys
→ SSH into EC2, store keys in ~/.aws/credentials or .env file
→ 
Problems:
  - Keys stored on disk → if EC2 hacked, attacker gets permanent keys
  - Keys don't expire → attacker has access until you manually rotate
  - Everyone on team now knows (or has seen) the keys
  - Keys in .env file → often accidentally committed to GitHub

THE RIGHT WAY (always do this):
EC2 instance needs to read from S3
→ Create IAM Role with S3 read permissions
→ Attach Role to EC2 instance
→ EC2 automatically gets temporary credentials via instance metadata
→ 
Benefits:
  - Credentials are temporary (expire in hours, auto-refreshed)
  - If EC2 is hacked, credentials expire soon anyway
  - No keys stored anywhere on disk
  - Zero code changes needed — AWS SDK automatically uses the role
### Common Role Examples

EC2 Instance Role:
  Trust: EC2 service can assume this role
  Policies: S3GetObject, DynamoDBFullAccess, CloudWatchLogsFullAccess

Lambda Execution Role:
  Trust: Lambda service can assume this role  
  Policies: AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole (CloudWatch logs)
            + whatever your function specifically needs

ECS Task Role:
  Trust: ECS tasks can assume this role
  Policies: specific services your container needs to call

Cross-Account Role:
  Trust: AWS account 111122223333 can assume this role
  Use case: Company A's team accesses Company B's resources
  Use case: CI/CD pipeline in one account deploys to another

CodePipeline Role:
  Trust: CodePipeline service can assume this role
  Policies: CodeBuild, S3, ECS deploy, CloudFormation

revisionPoints: → AWS services (EC2 accessing S3, Lambda writing to DynamoDB)
→ Applications running inside AWS
→ External users (federated from company Active Directory)
→ Other AWS accounts (cross-account access)
→ Web identity (users logged in with Google/Facebook via Cognito)
→ No password. No access keys. Auto-rotated. Much safer.
→ Create IAM user "app-service"
→ Generate access keys

sections.title: 📜 IAM Policies — The Core of Everything
sections.rawMarkdown:
> Policies are JSON documents that define what is ALLOWED or DENIED. Understanding policies is the difference between a junior and senior engineer.

### Policy Structure
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowS3ReadOnSpecificBucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:ListBucket"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-company-bucket",
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-company-bucket/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "aws:RequestedRegion": "us-east-1"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Every field explained:**

| Field | Values | Meaning |
|-------|--------|---------|
| `Version` | Always `"2012-10-17"` | Policy language version (don't change this) |
| `Sid` | Any string | Statement ID — optional label for humans |
| `Effect` | `"Allow"` or `"Deny"` | Explicitly allow or deny this action |
| `Action` | `"service:action"` | What AWS API call (e.g., `s3:GetObject`) |
| `Resource` | ARN or `"*"` | Which specific resource |
| `Principal` | Account/user/role ARN | WHO gets this (used in resource policies) |
| `Condition` | Key-value conditions | Only apply if condition is true |

### ARN — Amazon Resource Name
```
ARN format: arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resource

Examples:
arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket                    → S3 bucket (no region/account — global)
arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*                  → all objects inside bucket
arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456:instance/i-abc → specific EC2 instance
arn:aws:iam::123456789:user/alice          → IAM user (no region — global)
arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/LambdaRole    → IAM role
arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456:function:myFunc → Lambda function

"*" → wildcard (matches anything)
Use carefully — "Resource": "*" means ALL resources of that type
```

### Policy Evaluation Logic (Critical — Most Missed)

```
When you make an AWS API call, IAM evaluates in this ORDER:

1. EXPLICIT DENY → if ANY policy says Deny → DENIED. Stop. Final.
2. EXPLICIT ALLOW → if ANY policy says Allow (and no Deny) → ALLOWED
3. IMPLICIT DENY  → if nothing matches → DENIED (default)

DENY ALWAYS WINS over ALLOW. No exceptions.

Example:
  Group policy:  Allow s3:* on *  (full S3 access)
  User policy:   Deny s3:DeleteObject on *
  Result:        User can do ALL S3 actions EXCEPT delete
                 The Deny overrides the Allow for that specific action

ANOTHER EXAMPLE:
  User has: Allow ec2:* on *
  User has: No policy about S3
  Result for S3: DENIED (implicit deny — no policy = denied)
  Result for EC2: ALLOWED (explicit allow)
```

### Types of Policies

```
1. AWS MANAGED POLICIES
   Created and maintained by AWS. You cannot edit them.
   Examples: AmazonS3FullAccess, ReadOnlyAccess, AdministratorAccess
   Pros: always up-to-date when AWS releases new features
   Cons: often too permissive (broader than you need)
   Use: quick start, non-sensitive environments

2. CUSTOMER MANAGED POLICIES (recommended)
   You create them. You control them. Reusable across users/groups/roles.
   Best practice: create specific policies for specific needs
   Example: "AllowReadFromReportsBucketOnly"
   Version-controlled. Audit who changed what.

3. INLINE POLICIES
   Attached directly to ONE specific user/group/role
   Deleted when that user/group/role is deleted
   NOT reusable. Hard to audit at scale.
   Avoid in production. Use for: tightly coupled, one-off permissions.

4. RESOURCE-BASED POLICIES
   Attached to the RESOURCE (not the user)
   Examples: S3 Bucket Policy, SQS Queue Policy, Lambda Resource Policy
   Has a Principal field (who is being granted access)
   Allows cross-account access without assuming a role
   
   S3 Bucket Policy example — make bucket readable by everyone:
   {
     "Principal": "*",
     "Effect": "Allow",
     "Action": "s3:GetObject",
     "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-public-bucket/*"
   }
```

### Least Privilege — The Golden Rule

```
Give every user, role, and service the MINIMUM permissions 
required to do EXACTLY their job. Nothing more. Nothing ever.

WHY:
→ Hacker compromises developer account
  WITH least privilege:  can only access dev bucket, not prod DB
  WITHOUT least privilege: admin access → deletes everything, steals all data

HOW TO IMPLEMENT:
1. Start with ZERO permissions on every new entity
2. Add ONLY what is actually needed, one service at a time
3. Use specific ARNs (never "*" on Resource unless truly needed)
4. Review permissions quarterly — remove what's unused
5. Use IAM Access Analyzer to find overly permissive policies
6. Use IAM Access Advisor to see what permissions are actually used

EXAMPLE — Lambda that reads from one S3 bucket:
WRONG: AmazonS3FullAccess (can delete any bucket in account!)
RIGHT: 
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": ["s3:GetObject"],
  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-specific-bucket/*"
}
```

---

blocks.content:

### Policy Structure

**Every field explained:**

| Field | Values | Meaning |

| `Version` | Always `"2012-10-17"` | Policy language version (don't change this) |

revisionPoints: → Hacker compromises developer account

sections.title: 🔐 MFA — Multi-Factor Authentication
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
MFA = password you know + device you own

Even if password is stolen → attacker CANNOT login without the physical device.
This single control prevents 99% of account takeovers.

MFA DEVICE OPTIONS:
┌──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Virtual MFA App      │ Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password │
│                      │ 6-digit code changes every 30 seconds  │
│                      │ Most common, free, easy to set up      │
├──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FIDO Security Key    │ YubiKey — physical USB/NFC device      │
│                      │ Tap to authenticate. Most secure.      │
│                      │ Cannot be phished. Hardware-bound.     │
├──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Hardware TOTP Token  │ Physical device from Gemalto/RSA       │
│                      │ For air-gapped environments            │
└──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────┘

WHERE TO ENFORCE MFA:
✅ Root account: MANDATORY. Non-negotiable. First thing to do.
✅ All IAM users with console access: MANDATORY
✅ Privileged users (admins): MANDATORY
✅ Production deployments: enforce via SCP or IAM Condition

HOW TO ENFORCE MFA via IAM Policy:
Add this condition to any policy to require MFA:
"Condition": {
  "BoolIfExists": {
    "aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "true"
  }
}
→ If MFA not used → action denied, even with correct password
```

---

blocks.content:
MFA = password you know + device you own

Even if password is stolen → attacker CANNOT login without the physical device.
This single control prevents 99% of account takeovers.

MFA DEVICE OPTIONS:
┌──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Virtual MFA App      │ Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password │
│                      │ 6-digit code changes every 30 seconds  │
│                      │ Most common, free, easy to set up      │
├──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FIDO Security Key    │ YubiKey — physical USB/NFC device      │
│                      │ Tap to authenticate. Most secure.      │
│                      │ Cannot be phished. Hardware-bound.     │
├──────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Hardware TOTP Token  │ Physical device from Gemalto/RSA       │
│                      │ For air-gapped environments            │
└──────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────┘

WHERE TO ENFORCE MFA:
✅ Root account: MANDATORY. Non-negotiable. First thing to do.
✅ All IAM users with console access: MANDATORY
✅ Privileged users (admins): MANDATORY
✅ Production deployments: enforce via SCP or IAM Condition

HOW TO ENFORCE MFA via IAM Policy:
Add this condition to any policy to require MFA:
"Condition": {
  "BoolIfExists": {
    "aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "true"
  }
}
→ If MFA not used → action denied, even with correct password

revisionPoints: ✅ Root account: MANDATORY. Non-negotiable. First thing to do.
✅ All IAM users with console access: MANDATORY
✅ Privileged users (admins): MANDATORY
✅ Production deployments: enforce via SCP or IAM Condition
→ If MFA not used → action denied, even with correct password

sections.title: 💻 How to Access AWS (3 Ways)
sections.rawMarkdown:
### 1. AWS Management Console (Browser)
```
URL: https://ACCOUNT_ID.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
Auth: username + password (+ MFA if enabled)

Best for: learning, exploration, occasional tasks, monitoring dashboards
NOT for: automation, repeated tasks, CI/CD

Tip: Alias your account ID → settings → create account alias
     makes URL: https://mycompany.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
```

### 2. AWS CLI (Command Line)
```bash
# Install
brew install awscli                    # macOS
pip install awscli                     # Python
# or download from aws.amazon.com/cli

# Configure
aws configure
# Enter: Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Region, Output format

# Common commands
aws s3 ls                              # list buckets
aws ec2 describe-instances             # list EC2 instances
aws lambda list-functions              # list Lambda functions
aws iam list-users                     # list IAM users
aws logs tail /aws/lambda/myfunction   # stream Lambda logs

# Use profiles (multiple accounts)
aws configure --profile production
aws s3 ls --profile production

# Use role (assume role)
aws sts assume-role \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456:role/MyRole \
  --role-session-name my-session
```

### 3. AWS SDK (In Your Code)
```javascript
// JavaScript / Node.js
const { S3Client, GetObjectCommand } = require('@aws-sdk/client-s3');

// SDK automatically uses: IAM Role (if on EC2/Lambda) OR
// environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY) OR
// ~/.aws/credentials file

const client = new S3Client({ region: 'us-east-1' });
const response = await client.send(new GetObjectCommand({
  Bucket: 'my-bucket',
  Key: 'my-file.json'
}));
```

```python
# Python (boto3)
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-east-1')
response = s3.get_object(Bucket='my-bucket', Key='my-file.json')
```

```
SDK CREDENTIAL LOOKUP ORDER (automatic):
1. Explicit credentials in code (NEVER do this)
2. Environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY)
3. AWS CLI credentials file (~/.aws/credentials)
4. IAM Role attached to EC2/Lambda/ECS (BEST for production)
→ Always use IAM Roles in production — never hardcode keys
```

---

URL: https://ACCOUNT_ID.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
Auth: username + password (+ MFA if enabled)

Best for: learning, exploration, occasional tasks, monitoring dashboards
NOT for: automation, repeated tasks, CI/CD

Tip: Alias your account ID → settings → create account alias
     makes URL: https://mycompany.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
### 2. AWS CLI (Command Line)

# Install
brew install awscli                    # macOS
pip install awscli                     # Python
# or download from aws.amazon.com/cli

# Configure
aws configure
# Enter: Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Region, Output format

# Common commands
aws s3 ls                              # list buckets
aws ec2 describe-instances             # list EC2 instances
aws lambda list-functions              # list Lambda functions
aws iam list-users                     # list IAM users
aws logs tail /aws/lambda/myfunction   # stream Lambda logs

# Use profiles (multiple accounts)
aws configure --profile production
aws s3 ls --profile production

# Use role (assume role)
aws sts assume-role \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456:role/MyRole \
  --role-session-name my-session
### 3. AWS SDK (In Your Code)

# Python (boto3)
import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-east-1')
response = s3.get_object(Bucket='my-bucket', Key='my-file.json')

SDK CREDENTIAL LOOKUP ORDER (automatic):
1. Explicit credentials in code (NEVER do this)
2. Environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY)
3. AWS CLI credentials file (~/.aws/credentials)
4. IAM Role attached to EC2/Lambda/ECS (BEST for production)
→ Always use IAM Roles in production — never hardcode keys

revisionPoints: → Always use IAM Roles in production — never hardcode keys

sections.title: 🔬 IAM Advanced Concepts
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Permission Boundaries
```
A permission boundary = maximum permissions an identity can EVER have.
Even if you attach more permissive policies → boundary caps it.

USE CASE:
You want developers to create their own IAM roles (for their apps),
but you don't want them to create roles with MORE access than they themselves have.

Solution:
→ Set permission boundary on developer user
→ Any role they create is ALSO bounded by that boundary
→ Developer cannot accidentally (or maliciously) escalate privileges

{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": ["iam:CreateRole", "iam:AttachRolePolicy"],
  "Resource": "*",
  "Condition": {
    "StringEquals": {
      "iam:PermissionsBoundary": "arn:aws:iam::123456:policy/DevBoundary"
    }
  }
}
```

### IAM Conditions (Fine-Grained Control)
```
Conditions make policies context-aware. Examples:

ONLY allow from specific IP:
"Condition": {
  "IpAddress": {"aws:SourceIp": ["203.0.113.0/24"]}
}

ONLY allow if MFA was used:
"Condition": {
  "Bool": {"aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "true"}
}

ONLY allow during business hours (UTC):
"Condition": {
  "DateGreaterThan": {"aws:CurrentTime": "2024-01-01T09:00:00Z"},
  "DateLessThan": {"aws:CurrentTime": "2024-12-31T17:00:00Z"}
}

ONLY allow specific regions:
"Condition": {
  "StringEquals": {"aws:RequestedRegion": ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]}
}

ONLY allow with specific tag:
"Condition": {
  "StringEquals": {"ec2:ResourceTag/Environment": "dev"}
}
→ Developers can only start/stop EC2 instances tagged Environment=dev
```

### Service Control Policies (SCPs)
```
SCPs = IAM policies applied at the AWS ORGANIZATION level
→ Apply to entire AWS accounts (not individual users)
→ Cannot be overridden by anything inside the account — not even root!
→ Used by large enterprises with multiple AWS accounts

EXAMPLE USE CASES:
→ "No one in the dev account can delete CloudTrail logs"
→ "No one can create resources in regions outside us-east-1"
→ "No one can create IAM users with admin access"
→ "Everyone must use MFA for console access"

SCPs work differently from IAM policies:
→ SCP does NOT grant permissions (it just LIMITS what's possible)
→ You STILL need IAM policies to actually allow actions
→ Think of SCP as a jail — defines the walls
   IAM policies = keys that work inside the jail
```

### IAM Access Analyzer
```
Free service that analyzes resource policies to find:
→ S3 buckets accessible outside your account
→ IAM roles assumable by external accounts
→ Lambda functions with public access
→ SQS queues accessible to external accounts
→ KMS keys accessible externally

Reports: "This S3 bucket is accessible from account 999888777"
You decide: intended (external partner) → archive the finding
            not intended → fix the policy

ALSO does:
→ Policy validation (checks for syntax errors, typos in actions)
→ Unused access analysis (finds permissions you have but never use)
→ Policy generation (watch CloudTrail for 90 days → generate tight policy)
```

---

blocks.content: ### Permission Boundaries
### IAM Conditions (Fine-Grained Control)
### Service Control Policies (SCPs)

SCPs = IAM policies applied at the AWS ORGANIZATION level
→ Apply to entire AWS accounts (not individual users)
→ Cannot be overridden by anything inside the account — not even root!
→ Used by large enterprises with multiple AWS accounts

EXAMPLE USE CASES:
→ "No one in the dev account can delete CloudTrail logs"
→ "No one can create resources in regions outside us-east-1"
→ "No one can create IAM users with admin access"
→ "Everyone must use MFA for console access"

SCPs work differently from IAM policies:
→ SCP does NOT grant permissions (it just LIMITS what's possible)
→ You STILL need IAM policies to actually allow actions
→ Think of SCP as a jail — defines the walls
   IAM policies = keys that work inside the jail
### IAM Access Analyzer

Free service that analyzes resource policies to find:
→ S3 buckets accessible outside your account
→ IAM roles assumable by external accounts
→ Lambda functions with public access
→ SQS queues accessible to external accounts
→ KMS keys accessible externally

Reports: "This S3 bucket is accessible from account 999888777"
You decide: intended (external partner) → archive the finding
            not intended → fix the policy

ALSO does:
→ Policy validation (checks for syntax errors, typos in actions)
→ Unused access analysis (finds permissions you have but never use)
→ Policy generation (watch CloudTrail for 90 days → generate tight policy)

revisionPoints: → Set permission boundary on developer user
→ Any role they create is ALSO bounded by that boundary
→ Developer cannot accidentally (or maliciously) escalate privileges
→ Developers can only start/stop EC2 instances tagged Environment=dev
→ Apply to entire AWS accounts (not individual users)
→ Cannot be overridden by anything inside the account — not even root!
→ Used by large enterprises with multiple AWS accounts
→ "No one in the dev account can delete CloudTrail logs"

sections.title: ✅ IAM Best Practices Checklist
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
ACCOUNT SETUP:
☐ Enable MFA on root account (day 1, non-negotiable)
☐ Never create access keys for root account
☐ Create IAM admin user for daily use
☐ Enable IAM Access Analyzer in all regions

USERS:
☐ One IAM user per real person (no sharing)
☐ Enable MFA for ALL users with console access
☐ Use groups to assign permissions (not direct user policies)
☐ Review and remove inactive users quarterly

ACCESS KEYS:
☐ Never put access keys in code
☐ Never commit .env files with keys to version control
☐ Rotate access keys every 90 days
☐ Delete unused access keys immediately
☐ Use IAM roles instead of access keys wherever possible

POLICIES:
☐ Apply least privilege everywhere, always
☐ Use specific ARNs, not "*" on Resource when possible
☐ Use Customer Managed Policies (not inline) for reusability
☐ Review AWS Managed Policies before using (often too broad)
☐ Add Conditions for extra control (IP, MFA, region, tags)

SERVICES:
☐ Always use IAM Roles for EC2, Lambda, ECS, CodePipeline
☐ Use Secrets Manager for secrets (not environment variables)
☐ Enable CloudTrail in ALL regions (records all IAM activity)
☐ Set up alerting for: root login, new admin user, policy changes
```

---

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — IAM
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: What is the difference between an IAM User, Group, and Role?**
```
"IAM User is a permanent identity for a specific person or application.
Has their own credentials — password for console, access keys for CLI.
By default has zero permissions.

IAM Group is a collection of users. You attach policies to groups, not
individual users. Users inherit the group's permissions. Makes management
much easier — add a developer to the Developers group and they instantly
get all developer permissions.

IAM Role is a temporary identity that can be assumed by AWS services,
applications, or users from other accounts. Has no permanent credentials —
when assumed, AWS generates temporary credentials that auto-expire.
Use roles for: EC2 instances accessing S3, Lambda functions, cross-account,
anything AWS-to-AWS. Always prefer roles over access keys for services."
```

**Q: Why should you use IAM Roles for EC2 instead of storing access keys?**
```
"Two reasons: security and operational simplicity.

Security: if an access key is stored on EC2 and the instance is compromised,
the attacker has permanent credentials that work until you manually rotate them.
With IAM Roles, the credentials are temporary — they auto-expire every few hours
and auto-refresh. Even if the instance is fully compromised, the window of
exposure is limited.

Operational: with IAM Roles, there are no keys to manage, rotate, audit, or
accidentally commit to GitHub. The AWS SDK automatically finds the role
credentials from the instance metadata service. Zero code changes needed.
You can update the role's permissions without touching the EC2 instance at all."
```

**Q: A Deny policy and an Allow policy exist for the same action. What happens?**
```
"Deny wins. Always. Without exception.

IAM evaluation order:
1. Explicit DENY → denied immediately, evaluation stops
2. Explicit ALLOW → allowed (if no deny exists)
3. Nothing matches → implicitly denied (default is deny everything)

The only way to allow an action is: explicit Allow AND no Deny.
This is why Deny statements are powerful — they're the final word.
Use this for guardrails: 'No one can delete CloudTrail, period.'
Even if someone has AdministratorAccess, a Deny for CloudTrail deletion wins."
```

**Q: How would you design IAM for a startup with 20 developers, 5 DevOps, 2 admins?**
```
"Four groups:
  Admins group: AdministratorAccess (2 people, both with MFA enforced)
  DevOps group: EC2, ECS, CodePipeline, CloudFormation, CloudWatch
  Developers group: specific services only — Lambda, S3 (dev buckets only),
                   RDS (dev databases), CloudWatch logs
  ReadOnly group: for auditors, contractors, view-only access

Create individual IAM users for each person, MFA enforced for all.
All production service accounts use IAM Roles (not IAM users with keys).
Enable IAM Access Analyzer. CloudTrail in all regions.
Set up alerting for: console login without MFA, root account usage,
new admin-level policy attachment, any access to prod from non-prod accounts.

If company grows > 50 people: move to AWS SSO (Identity Center) —
single login for all AWS accounts, integrated with corporate directory."
```

**Q: You discover an IAM access key was committed to a public GitHub repo. What do you do?**
```
"Treat this as an active security incident. Immediate response:

T+0 minutes:
→ DEACTIVATE the access key in IAM console immediately (takes 5 seconds)
→ This stops any ongoing unauthorized use instantly

T+5 minutes:
→ Open CloudTrail → filter by the compromised access key ID
→ Look at ALL API calls made with that key in the past 90 days
→ Specifically: look for CreateUser, AttachPolicy, PutBucketPolicy (signs of escalation)
→ Check which regions, which services, unusual times

T+15 minutes:
→ Create new access key for the affected user (if legitimate use was needed)
→ Update any applications that were using the old key
→ Revoke any resources created by the attacker (EC2 instances, etc.)

T+30 minutes:
→ Remove the key from GitHub history (git filter-branch or BFG Repo Cleaner)
→ Force-push to GitHub
→ GitHub has their own token scanning — notify them too

Prevention going forward:
→ Add pre-commit hooks (git-secrets, truffleHog) that block key commits
→ Enable AWS Config rule for 'iam-no-inline-policy' and similar
→ Enable GuardDuty — it would have alerted 'Credentials used from unusual location'
→ Consider: do CI/CD pipelines even need user access keys? Switch to OIDC roles."
```

**Q: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you implement it at scale?**
```
"Least privilege: every identity gets the minimum permissions needed to do
their job — nothing more, nothing less.

At scale, implementing it is a process:

1. Policy generation: use IAM Access Analyzer to watch real usage for 90 days,
   then generate a policy based on what was actually called. Much tighter than guessing.

2. Permission boundaries: for developer self-service, let them create roles but
   with a boundary that caps what those roles can do.

3. Tag-based access control: 'developers can only access resources tagged with
   Environment=dev' — one policy, scales to hundreds of resources.

4. Regular reviews: IAM Access Advisor shows last time each permission was used.
   Remove permissions unused for 90+ days.

5. Service Control Policies at org level: hard limits that can't be bypassed.
   'No one in any account can disable CloudTrail' — even admins.

The goal isn't perfection on day one. It's a culture of progressively tightening
access over time, combined with tooling that makes overly-broad permissions visible."
```

---

→ Enable GuardDuty — it would have alerted 'Credentials used from unusual location'
→ Consider: do CI/CD pipelines even need user access keys? Switch to OIDC roles."

qaItems.question: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you implement it at scale?
qaItems.answer:
"Least privilege: every identity gets the minimum permissions needed to do
their job — nothing more, nothing less.

At scale, implementing it is a process:

1. Policy generation: use IAM Access Analyzer to watch real usage for 90 days,
   then generate a policy based on what was actually called. Much tighter than guessing.

2. Permission boundaries: for developer self-service, let them create roles but
   with a boundary that caps what those roles can do.

3. Tag-based access control: 'developers can only access resources tagged with
   Environment=dev' — one policy, scales to hundreds of resources.

4. Regular reviews: IAM Access Advisor shows last time each permission was used.
   Remove permissions unused for 90+ days.

5. Service Control Policies at org level: hard limits that can't be bypassed.
   'No one in any account can disable CloudTrail' — even admins.

The goal isn't perfection on day one. It's a culture of progressively tightening
access over time, combined with tooling that makes overly-broad permissions visible."

revisionPoints: → DEACTIVATE the access key in IAM console immediately (takes 5 seconds)
→ This stops any ongoing unauthorized use instantly
→ Open CloudTrail → filter by the compromised access key ID
→ Look at ALL API calls made with that key in the past 90 days
→ Specifically: look for CreateUser, AttachPolicy, PutBucketPolicy (signs of escalation)
→ Check which regions, which services, unusual times
→ Create new access key for the affected user (if legitimate use was needed)
→ Update any applications that were using the old key

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
IAM connects to EVERY AWS service. Literally every one.
You cannot use any AWS service without IAM allowing it.

SPECIFICALLY:
→ EC2: needs an IAM Role to access S3, DynamoDB, etc.
→ Lambda: needs an IAM Execution Role to write logs, read SQS
→ S3: uses Bucket Policies (resource-based IAM) for access control
→ RDS: IAM authentication available (instead of DB password)
→ CodePipeline: IAM Roles to deploy to EC2, ECS, Lambda
→ CloudTrail: records every IAM API call (audit trail)
→ Organizations + SCPs: org-level IAM guardrails

NEXT TOPIC: EC2 — the first actual compute service.
You'll create an IAM Role for EC2 in the very first EC2 lesson.
```

---

---
